1: ταῦτα ἐλάλησεν — "these things he spoke" — aorist, closing the farewell discourse (chs. 14–16) before the prayer begins. ἐπάρας τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτοῦ εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν — "having lifted his eyes toward heaven" — a posture of direct address attested in synagogue prayer and the Psalms; Jesus speaks to the Father face-to-face, not through an intermediary formula. ἐλήλυθεν ἡ ὥρα — "the hour has come" — perfect tense, elēlythen: the arrival of the hour is a completed event whose results now stand. This is the hour repeatedly deferred in the Gospel (2:4; 7:30; 8:20) and now declared present. The two doxazō imperatives — "glorify your Son, that the Son may glorify you" — are aorist, marking the mutual glorification as a single completed act, not an ongoing disposition.
2: καθὼς ἔδωκας αὐτῷ ἐξουσίαν πάσης σαρκός — "just as you gave him authority over all flesh." Exousia (G1849) is delegated authority — a granted right, not mere capacity. Pasēs sarkos — "all flesh" — the Semitic idiom for all humanity without exception (cf. Gen 6:12; Isa 40:5). The scope is universal; the exercise is particular: "so that to all those you have given him, he might give eternal life." δέδωκας (perfect, "you have given") — the giving stands as a completed and present reality; Jesus prays over an already-constituted people.
3: αὕτη δέ ἐστιν ἡ αἰώνιος ζωή — "And this is eternal life." The Evangelist — or Jesus within the prayer — offers a formal definition, a rare move in the Gospel. αἰώνιος (G166, dispute_level 3): the adjective derives from aiōn (age/era), and carries both qualitative ("of the age to come," the divine life-order) and quantitative ("without end") senses. The MKT renders it "eternal" but the qualitative dimension is primary: this is the life of the new age, not merely endless duration. ἵνα γινώσκωσίν σε — "that they may know you" — present subjunctive, ongoing knowing, not a single act of recognition. Ginōskō in John carries relational freight: the same word used for the knowing between Father and Son (10:15), and between husband and wife in the LXX. τὸν μόνον ἀληθινὸν θεόν — "the only true God" — alēthinos (G228) means "real/genuine" as against counterfeit; not merely the only God numerically, but the God who is authentically what God is.
4: ἐγώ σε ἐδόξασα ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς — "I glorified you on the earth" — aorist, looking back over the entire ministry as a completed act. The initiative is Jesus's: he brought glory to the Father, not the reverse. τὸ ἔργον τελειώσας — "having completed/perfected the work" — aorist participle of teleioō (G5048), to bring to its destined end. The same root appears in the great cry from the cross, tetelestai (19:30): what is here prayed in anticipation ("having finished the work you gave me") is declared accomplished at death. The prayer is proleptic — Jesus speaks of the cross as already complete.
5: νῦν δόξασόν με σύ, πάτερ, παρὰ σεαυτῷ τῇ δόξῃ ᾗ εἶχον — "And now, Father, glorify me alongside yourself with the glory that I had." Para seautō — "alongside yourself / in your presence" — the preposition denotes proximity and personal relation, not merely location. πρὸ τοῦ τὸν κόσμον εἶναι παρὰ σοί — "before the world was, alongside you" — the pre-existence of the Son stated explicitly: the glory requested is a return to a state that antedated creation. The construction pro tou + infinitive marks temporal priority: the Son's shared glory precedes cosmogony. This is the most direct statement of the Son's pre-existence in the prayer and one of the clearest in the Gospel (cf. 1:1; 8:58).
6: ἐφανέρωσά σου τὸ ὄνομα τοῖς ἀνθρώποις — "I revealed your name to the people." Phaneroō (G5319, aorist) is the Evangelist's verb for making visible what is otherwise concealed (cf. 1:31; 2:11; 9:3). "Your name" in Jewish understanding is not a label but a disclosure of character and presence — the divine shem. Jesus claims to have made the Father's name-reality visible through his ministry. οὓς δέδωκάς μοι ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου — "those you gave me out of the world" — perfect, the Father's giving is a standing reality. τὸν λόγον σου τετήρηκαν — "they have kept your word" — perfect, their keeping also stands. Two perfects balance each other: the gift given and the reception completed.
7: νῦν ἔγνωκαν ὅτι πάντα ὅσα δέδωκάς μοι παρὰ σοῦ εἰσίν — "Now they have known that all things, whatever you have given me, are from you." Egnōkan — perfect of ginōskō: they have arrived at and now rest in this knowledge. The insight is singular: everything Jesus has — his words, works, authority, disciples — originates from the Father. The chain of origin (Father → Son → disciples) is the disciples's growing theological understanding that the prayer consolidates.
8: τὰ ῥήματα ἃ ἔδωκάς μοι δέδωκα αὐτοῖς — "the words you gave me I have given to them." The distinction between rhēma (individual spoken word/utterance) and logos (the Word as self-disclosure or teaching body) matters here: rhēmata are the specific verbal utterances transmitted down the chain. ἔλαβον (aorist, "they received"), ἔγνωσαν (aorist, "they knew"), ἐπίστευσαν (aorist, "they believed") — three aorists in sequence mark three completed acts that together constitute the disciples' reception: they received the words, recognized their origin, and committed to it by faith.
9: οὐ περὶ τοῦ κόσμου ἐρωτῶ — "I am not praying for the world." The present tense erōtō ("I am asking") marks this as the active scope of the prayer at this moment, not an absolute exclusion — the Evangelist has already shown Jesus speaking of his love for the world (3:16; 12:32). The restriction is strategic: the prayer focuses on those already given to him, the visible community who will carry the mission. περὶ ὧν δέδωκάς μοι — "for those you have given me" — perfect, the given ones are constituted. σοί εἰσιν — "they are yours" — predicate without emphasis; their belonging to the Father is the ground of the prayer.
10: καὶ τὰ ἐμὰ πάντα σά ἐστιν καὶ τὰ σὰ ἐμά — "and all mine is yours, and yours mine." The mutual possession is stated with remarkable compression — no verb in the second clause, the reciprocity made absolute. This mutual ownership of all things is the ontological ground of the Son's prayer: he prays as one who shares everything with the Father. δεδόξασμαι ἐν αὐτοῖς — "I have been glorified in them" — perfect passive: the disciples are already the locus where the Son's glory has landed and remains visible. The prayer is not for glory not yet given but for the protection of a glory already present in a fragile vessel.
11: Ἅγιε πάτερ — "Holy Father" — a unique address in the NT. Jesus elsewhere addresses God simply as Pater or Abba; the addition of Hagie (G40, "holy") creates a title that invokes the divine attribute that most fully belongs to God alone. Holiness here grounds the petition: the one who is holy is asked to guard those set apart for him. τήρησον αὐτοὺς ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί σου — "keep them in/by your name" — tēreō (G5083) is "guard/keep watch over" — protective keeping, not mere maintenance. ἵνα ὦσιν ἕν καθὼς ἡμεῖς — "that they may be one as we are." Hen — neuter, "one thing" — unity of kind, not numerical identity (which would be heis, masculine). The grammar preserves both unity and distinction in the divine relationship, and the disciples' unity is modeled on it, not identical to it.
12: ἐγὼ ἐτήρουν αὐτοὺς ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί σου — "I was keeping them in your name" — imperfect, continuous past action: the keeping was Jesus's ongoing activity throughout the ministry. οὐδεὶς ἐξ αὐτῶν ἀπώλετο εἰ μὴ ὁ υἱὸς τῆς ἀπωλείας — "none of them was lost except the son of destruction." Apōleia (G684) — destruction, perdition — gives Judas his epithet: he is defined by the outcome, the one who was heading toward destruction. The exception clause (ei mē) does not cancel the claim of keeping; it marks Judas as outside the group being described. ἵνα ἡ γραφὴ πληρωθῇ — "that the scripture might be fulfilled" — the fulfillment formula anchors the loss of Judas within providential necessity; the Evangelist probably has Ps 41:9 in view (cf. 13:18).
13: νῦν δὲ πρὸς σὲ ἔρχομαι — "But now I am coming to you" — present tense, proleptic: Jesus speaks of the cross-and-return as an imminent present movement. ἵνα ἔχωσιν τὴν χαρὰν τὴν ἐμὴν πεπληρωμένην ἐν ἑαυτοῖς — "that they may have my joy fulfilled in themselves." Peplerōmenēn — perfect passive participle, "having been made full/complete": the joy the disciples are to share is not an installment but the complete measure of Jesus's own joy. The farewell discourse in ch. 16 promised that joy-in-sorrow would be converted to a joy no one can take; this prayer confirms the goal.
14: ἐγὼ δέδωκα αὐτοῖς τὸν λόγον σου — "I have given them your word" — perfect, the giving stands. ὁ κόσμος ἐμίσησεν αὐτούς — "the world hated them" — aorist, the hatred is a historical response already incurred. ὅτι οὐκ εἰσὶν ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου καθὼς ἐγὼ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου — "because they are not out of the world just as I am not out of the world." The ek preposition marks source or origin: the disciples no longer derive their identity and orientation from the world-system, just as Jesus does not. This is the theological ground of the world's hostility.
15: οὐκ ἐρωτῶ ἵνα ἄρῃς αὐτοὺς ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου — "I am not asking that you take them out of the world." The prayer explicitly refuses withdrawal. ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα τηρήσῃς αὐτοὺς ἐκ τοῦ πονηροῦ — "but that you keep them from the evil one." Tou ponērou — genitive of ponēros (G4190): the masculine form is decisive — this is "the evil one" (a person), not "evil" (an abstract). The same construction appears in the Lord's Prayer (Matt 6:13). The petition is not for isolation but for guarded engagement: in the world but protected from the one who rules it (cf. 12:31; 14:30; 16:11).
16: ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου οὐκ εἰσίν, καθὼς ἐγὼ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου οὐκ εἰμί — verbatim repetition of v. 14b. In the Evangelist's style, repetition is rarely decorative; it marks the thesis being held constant while the surrounding argument develops. Here the repeated declaration of the disciples' non-world-derivation frames the petition of v. 15 on both sides, ensuring the reader understands the basis for the protection request.
17: ἁγίασον αὐτοὺς ἐν τῇ ἀληθείᾳ — "Sanctify them in/by the truth." Hagiazō (G37, aorist imperative): the verb's semantic range spans ceremonial consecration (setting apart for holy use), ritual purification, and moral transformation. All three are operative here: the disciples are being set apart from the world-system (v. 16), purified for service, and morally shaped by what is true. ὁ λόγος ὁ σὸς ἀλήθειά ἐστιν — "your word is truth." Not "true" (alēthēs) but "truth" (alētheia) without the article — the word does not merely correspond to truth; it is the substance of truth. The Evangelist has already identified Jesus as "the truth" (14:6); now the Father's word shares that characterization.
18: καθὼς ἐμὲ ἀπέστειλας εἰς τὸν κόσμον, κἀγὼ ἀπέστειλα αὐτοὺς εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "Just as you sent me into the world, I also sent them into the world." The sending of the disciples is explicitly modeled on the Father's sending of the Son: same verb (apostellō, G649), same direction (into the world), same syntax. This is the Fourth Gospel's theological grounding of mission: the disciples' apostolate participates in the structure of the divine sending, not merely in its content. The aorist "I also sent" treats the commissioning as already accomplished — cf. 20:21.
19: ὑπὲρ αὐτῶν ἐγὼ ἁγιάζω ἐμαυτόν — "for their sake I sanctify myself." The formula is priestly and sacrificial: the high priest consecrates himself before presenting an offering on behalf of others. Jesus is here both priest and offering — he sets himself apart for the purpose of his own sacrifice. Hagiazō emauton — the reflexive "sanctify myself" — occurs nowhere else in the NT; it is a unique claim. ἵνα ὦσιν καὶ αὐτοὶ ἡγιασμένοι ἐν ἀληθείᾳ — "that they too may be sanctified in truth" — perfect passive participle hēgiasmenoi: the result of his self-consecration is their standing consecration, an accomplished state, not merely an ongoing process. En alētheia here is probably instrumental: by means of truth (the word, cf. v. 17), and/or in genuine reality (as against merely ceremonial consecration).
20: Οὐ περὶ τούτων δὲ ἐρωτῶ μόνον — "I do not pray only for these." The scope of the prayer expands beyond the immediate disciples to encompass all future believers. ἀλλὰ καὶ περὶ τῶν πιστευόντων διὰ τοῦ λόγου αὐτῶν — "but also for those who believe through their word." Dia tou logou autōn — through their word/message: the chain of transmission is explicit — Father → Son → disciples → their word → future believers. The present participle pisteuontōn ("those believing") includes all who come to faith through the apostolic message, which by the Evangelist's own account (20:31) includes the Gospel itself.
21: ἵνα πάντες ἕν ὦσιν — "that all may be one." Hen — neuter, as in v. 11 — the same grammatical form maintains consistency: the unity prayed for is participatory, not numerical. καθὼς σύ, πάτερ, ἐν ἐμοί, κἀγὼ ἐν σοί — "just as you, Father, are in me and I in you" — the mutual indwelling (perikhōrēsis in later theological terminology) is the model, not just the analogy. ἵνα καὶ αὐτοὶ ἐν ἡμῖν ὦσιν — "that they also may be in us" — the goal is believers' participation in the Trinitarian fellowship, not mere social unity. ἵνα ὁ κόσμος πιστεύῃ — the unity of believers is missionary in purpose: it is to be visible enough to convince the world of the Son's divine sending.
22: κἀγὼ τὴν δόξαν ἣν δέδωκάς μοι δέδωκα αὐτοῖς — "And I have given them the glory that you have given me." Both verbs are perfect: the Father's giving of glory to the Son and the Son's giving of that glory to the disciples are both completed and standing. The glory given to the disciples is not merely honor or reputation but the self-revealing radiance of the divine presence — the same doxa that the Evangelist says "we beheld" (1:14). ἵνα ὦσιν ἕν καθὼς ἡμεῖς ἕν — "that they may be one as we are one" — neuter throughout; the unity given by glory is qualitative, not numerical fusion.
23: ἐγὼ ἐν αὐτοῖς καὶ σὺ ἐν ἐμοί, ἵνα ὦσιν τετελειωμένοι εἰς ἕν — "I in them and you in me, that they may be perfected into one." Teteleiōmenoi eis hen — perfect passive participle with eis of goal or result: "brought to completion into oneness." The verb teleioō (G5048) carries its characteristic Johannine sense of completing, bringing to intended purpose. The unity is not static but the telos toward which the community is being directed. ἵνα γινώσκῃ ὁ κόσμος ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας — "so that the world may know that you sent me" — the missionary claim again: the visible completion of the community is the apologetic argument for the Son's divine origin.
24: θέλω — "I will / I desire" — not the prayer-verb erōtaō used throughout (vv. 9, 15, 20) but the volitional thelō (G2309): "I will it, I want it." The shift marks this verse as something other than petition — it is a statement of the Son's determined will addressed to the Father. ἵνα ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγὼ κἀκεῖνοι ὦσιν μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ — "that where I am, they may also be with me" — the eschatological goal: the disciples' presence with Jesus where he is (cf. 14:3: "that where I am you may also be"). ἵνα θεωρῶσιν τὴν δόξαν τὴν ἐμήν — "that they may behold my glory" — theōreō (G2334), the seeing of prolonged direct observation, not a glance. The glory is the pre-existent glory of v. 5. The petition is for the disciples' eschatological vision of what they could not fully see during the ministry.
25: Πάτερ δίκαιε — "Righteous Father" — a second unique address in the prayer, balancing "Holy Father" in v. 11. Dikaios (G1342): just, righteous — the attribute that governs v. 25's claim: God's justice will vindicate the disciples' knowledge of Jesus over against the world's ignorance. ὁ κόσμος σε οὐκ ἔγνω — "the world did not know you" — aorist, a settled verdict on the world's history of not-knowing. ἐγὼ δέ σε ἔγνων — "but I knew you" — same aorist, a simple historical fact about the Son's consistent knowing of the Father. καὶ οὗτοι ἔγνωσαν ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας — "and these knew that you sent me" — the disciples stand with the Son in knowing, differentiated from the world.
26: καὶ ἐγνώρισα αὐτοῖς τὸ ὄνομά σου καὶ γνωρίσω — "and I have made your name known to them, and I will make it known." The movement from aorist (egnōrisa) to future (gnōrisō) is significant: Jesus's disclosure of the Father's name is both a completed ministry achievement and a continuing work — the making-known will continue through the Spirit (cf. 16:13–15) and through the community's witness. ἵνα ἡ ἀγάπη ἣν ἠγάπησάς με ἐν αὐτοῖς ᾖ κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτοῖς — "that the love with which you loved me may be in them, and I in them." The prayer closes with the ultimate goal: the Father's love for the Son is to take up residence in the disciples. Agapē (G26) here is not the disciples' love but the divine love itself — the inner-Trinitarian love — relocating into the believing community. The final phrase "and I in them" is the mechanism: Christ's indwelling is how the divine love enters and remains.
